name: RepoGraph
maintainer: Siru Ouyang et al. (University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign, Tencent AI Seattle Lab, Rice University, University of Notre Dame)
license: [uncertain] — not confirmed from sources; likely MIT based on academic OSS norms
last_major_release: [uncertain] — no versioned release tags found; code published October 2024 alongside ICLR 2025 paper submission
activity: ~243 stars, ~37 forks as of early 2026; low commit velocity post-paper-release (research prototype); ~3 open issues; single primary author (ozyyshr); effectively maintenance mode
storage_backend: Filesystem (in-memory graph during construction, serialized to JSON or binary cache on disk); no database backend
representation_primitive: Code-AST + entity+edge. Each node is a single line of code; edges represent definition-reference relationships between lines. The graph G = {V, E} uses code lines as the fundamental unit, making it finer-grained than file-level or function-level representations. Sub-graphs (k-hop ego-graphs) are extracted around search terms (function/class names) and serialized as structured context strings for LLM consumption.
api_shape: Python library; no MCP server; no REST API. Integration is via direct Python import — callers invoke RepoGraph to build the graph and retrieve ego-subgraphs. CLI wrapper included for SWE-bench evaluation scripts.
hosting: self-host only; research prototype, no managed offering
llm_dependency: Retrieval only — LLM is used at inference time to consume the retrieved ego-graph context. Graph construction (ingest) is purely static/AST-based, requiring no LLM calls. Evaluation used GPT-4o and DeepSeek-Coder as backbone solvers; RepoGraph itself is model-agnostic.
schema_extensibility: fixed — the schema (nodes=code-lines, edges=def/ref relationships) is hard-coded and determined by the AST parser. No configuration file or LLM-driven schema extension is supported.
schema_richness: Minimal schema by design: 1 node type (code-line), 2 edge types (definition-link, reference-link). Deliberately not a multi-entity KG. No multi-label, no multi-graph. Far from Gimle §5's target of 20+ entity types / 15+ edge types — this is a structural navigation index, not a semantic knowledge graph.
temporal_model: none — snapshot only. The graph is built once per repository state (typically at the commit being repaired). No versioning, no valid_from/to timestamps, no event log. Re-run from scratch to reflect new commits.
forgetting_policy: none — full rebuild required to update; no incremental invalidation
ingest_latency_class: batch-offline — graph construction involves full AST parsing of the entire repository, typically seconds to minutes depending on repo size
retrieval_modes: graph-traversal (k-hop ego-graph expansion from keyword-matched anchor nodes). No vector similarity, no BM25. Search terms are extracted from the issue/task description and used to identify anchor nodes; then the k-hop neighborhood is returned as structured context.
retrieval_agenticity: static-lookup with mild heuristic (keyword-to-anchor matching). The k parameter for hop depth is configurable but fixed per call. No HyDE, no Self-RAG, no multi-step reasoning over the graph — retrieval is a single-pass sub-graph extraction.
retrieval_budget_control: yes, partially — k (hop count) is a parameter; the number of top-k results returned is configurable. No explicit token-budget or cost-budget as a first-class API parameter.
multi_project_namespace: none — designed for single-repository use per instantiation. No cross-project namespace partitioning.
multi_user_access: none — single-user research tool; no access control
embedder_options: none — RepoGraph is entirely embedding-free. Retrieval is structural (graph traversal from lexical keyword match), not semantic/vector.
llm_client_options: none built-in — RepoGraph produces structured text context; the caller's LLM client consumes it. No LLM client is bundled.
cost_privacy: Zero token cost for ingest/graph-build (no LLM calls). Retrieval cost is borne by whatever LLM the caller uses to consume the retrieved context. All code stays on-host; no data leaves the machine during graph construction.
code_artifact_linkage: line-level-defs+refs (primary and unique strength). Every source line is a node; definition and reference edges connect lines across files. Retrieval returns k-hop ego-graphs centred on matched function/class names. This is the finest-grained linkage among the three papers surveyed. No issue-PR-to-symbol chain, no AST-node-level semantic typing beyond line identity.
code_analysis_features: Symbol index (first-party, via AST parsing — functions and classes are anchor-node candidates). Definition/reference graph (first-party). Cross-file dependency tracking (first-party). No call graph in the traditional sense (edges are def/ref not call/called-by). No diff-awareness. No import resolution beyond reference edges. No type-awareness. Python-first; multi-language depends on Tree-sitter parser availability.
memory_os_layer: plaintext — constructs a static structural context document that is injected into the LLM prompt. No parametric (weight) tuning, no activation/KV-cache reuse.
self_improvement_mechanism: none — static tool; no feedback loop from repair outcomes to graph construction
provenance_standard: none — no audit trail; graph edges carry no provenance metadata
benchmark_results: SWE-bench (full, verified): +32.8% average relative improvement across four baselines (Agentless + SWE-agent, two framework types). CrossCodeEval: improvement on both EM and ES metrics with GPT-4o and DeepSeek-Coder backbones (exact numbers in Table 5 of the paper). ICLR 2025 accepted paper.
strengths: 1. Finest-grained code linkage available (line-level def/ref graph), giving downstream LLMs precise navigation context unavailable from file-level or function-level indices. 2. Plug-in design — works with any existing agent or procedural SWE framework without requiring framework changes; +32.8% average relative gain across all four tested baselines. 3. Zero embedding/LLM cost at ingest — purely deterministic AST construction means no API spend and no privacy leakage during context-building.
weaknesses: 1. No temporal model — cannot track how code evolves across commits; schema must be rebuilt from scratch on each snapshot, making it unsuitable for live incremental indexing. 2. Schema is too narrow for Gimle §5 — only line nodes and def/ref edges; no concept of issues, PRs, modules, decisions, or any cross-domain entity. Cannot answer 'which issue caused this line to change.' 3. Research prototype status — low ongoing activity, no versioned releases, no MCP integration, and no managed hosting; production adoption requires significant engineering investment.
fit_with_gimle_section_5: Verdict: steal-specific-ideas. Ideas to steal: (1) Line-level definition-reference edge schema for the Symbol layer of Gimle §5. RepoGraph's edge model maps cleanly onto Gimle's File→Symbol containment + Symbol→Symbol reference edges: a `DEFINES` edge from a file-line to a symbol node, and `REFERENCES` edges between symbol nodes. Example schema snippet to adopt: `(:Symbol {name, kind, file_path, line_start, line_end})-[:REFERENCES]->(:Symbol)` and `(:File)-[:DEFINES]->(:Symbol)`. (2) k-hop ego-graph retrieval as a retrieval primitive: when an agent queries Gimle's graph for context around a symbol, the same k-hop traversal logic can bound the returned subgraph size. Incompatibilities: the line-as-node model does not compose with Gimle's bi-temporal requirement (valid_from/to on every node/edge), and the absence of any issue/PR/decision entity type means RepoGraph's schema cannot serve as the primary Gimle §5 foundation — only as the code-symbol sublayer.
